question: What did stakeholders recommend about credential recognition for internationally trained physicians?
answer: Stakeholders recommended scaling up Practice-Ready Assessment programs to allow experienced international physicians to practice sooner [hesa451_impact_of_immigration_policy_0018]. The Canadian Medical Association called for streamlining licensing pathways that do not compromise standards of care and providing targeted resources to provinces and territories to expand assessment capacity [hesa451_impact_of_immigration_policy_0018]. World Education Services recommended that the federal government establish frameworks and funding for modernized registration processes and supervised clinical experience models tailored to specific health occupations [hesa451_impact_of_immigration_policy_0023].

passages.text: When newcomers are included in our health workforce, staffing gaps begin to close. Another, a UK-trained specialist with 27 years of distinguished experience, still cannot qualify for permanent residency — a frustrating barrier that undermines both his contributions and Canada's healthcare capacity. Scaling up proven expedited licensing pathways that do not compromise standards of care is essential.

metadata.study_or_consultation_title: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals

passages.text: Establish federal frameworks and provide funding to provincial stakeholders to implement and/or expand proven practices, including modernized registration processes, supervised clinical experience and assessment models, and career navigation supports tailored to specific occupations.

metadata.study_or_consultation_title: Impact of Immigration Policy on Healthcare and Barriers to Integrating Internationally Trained Professionals

passages.text: In 2023/2024, there were 2,402 visa fellow trainees providing high-level clinical care. It is estimated that there are 13,000 foreign-trained physicians in Canada who are not practicing medicine; some may be unable to practice because their foreign training does not satisfy Canadian standards.

metadata.org: Association of Faculties of Medicine of Canada
